name: Neuroradiology

toc.text: Normal Variants vs. Pathology — High-Yield Distinctions

html:
Neuroradiology

Reading a Pediatric Brain MRI

Pediatric neuroimaging differs from adult neuroimaging in one fundamental respect: the brain is a moving target. Myelination, cortical thickness, gray-white differentiation, and ventricular size all change dramatically across the first two years of life. Every MRI must be interpreted relative to the child's age — a finding that is pathological at 18 months may be normal at 2 weeks, and vice versa.

General Reporting Structure

DOMAINWHAT TO ASSESS
Ventricles and CSF spacesSize, symmetry, configuration; transependymal flow; choroid plexus; aqueduct patency
Brain parenchyma — white matterSignal relative to age; distribution of abnormality; myelination stage
Brain parenchyma — gray matterCortical thickness and gyral pattern; basal ganglia and thalami signal; hippocampi
Extra-axial spacesSubarachnoid spaces (symmetric? Blood?); subdural collections; epidural
Vascular structuresArterial flow voids; venous sinuses; enhancement pattern if contrast given
Posterior fossaCerebellar hemispheres and vermis; brainstem morphology; 4th ventricle; cisterns
Skull base and adjacent structuresCraniovertebral junction; skull base foramina; cranial nerves if dedicated sequences; sinuses and mastoids

Sequence-by-Sequence Review

Review sequences in pairs. The table below gives the primary question each sequence answers, what to look for, and the key pediatric pitfall.

Table 1. MRI Sequence Review Guide

SEQUENCEPRIMARY QUESTIONWHAT TO LOOK FORKEY PEDIATRIC PITFALLCLINICAL UTILITY
Sagittal T1Is the anatomy normal?Corpus callosum (genu→splenium); brainstem morphology; cerebellar vermis; tonsil position; pituitary; aqueduct; CV junctionCorpus callosum thinning vs. agenesis vs. hypogenesis — identify what is present (genu, body, splenium, rostrum) before calling partial agenesisStructural overview; midline anomalies; Chiari
Axial T1Gray-white differentiation; is myelination appropriate for age?Myelination pattern (compare to expected for age); basal ganglia; cortical ribbon; ventricle size/symmetry; extra-axial spacesMyelination is T1-bright before T2-dark — up to ~18 months T1 leads T2. Do not call delayed myelination without knowing the child's age.Myelination staging; gray-white differentiation; basal ganglia signal
Axial T2Where is the signal abnormality?White matter signal (hyper/hypo); basal ganglia/thalami; brainstem; CSF spaces; vascular flow voids; sinuses/mastoidsUnmyelinated white matter is T2-bright normally — always interpret white matter signal relative to age. Thalami are T2-bright in neonates normally.Broad signal survey; leukodystrophy; edema; malformation
FLAIRIs there cortical or juxtacortical pathology?Periventricular/deep/subcortical WM lesions; cortical signal; subarachnoid spaces (blood/infection); perivascular spacesFLAIR is unreliable in children <2 years — CSF suppression is incomplete due to immature myelination. Do not over-read FLAIR in infants.MS/demyelination; cortical dysplasia; subarachnoid hemorrhage; encephalitis
DWI / ADCIs there acute restriction?Acute infarct (high DWI + low ADC); abscess (ring-enhancing + restricted center); epidermoid (high DWI); T2 shine-through (high DWI + high ADC)T2 shine-through mimics restriction — ALWAYS confirm on ADC map. Restricted diffusion in neonates may be physiologic in unmyelinated regions.Stroke; cytotoxic edema; abscess; HIE DWI timing (peaks 3–5 days)
SWI / GREIs there blood, calcium, or iron?Microhemorrhages; cavernomas; hemorrhagic transformation; calcifications (hypointense on SWI); iron deposition; venous anatomyCalcifications and blood products are both hypointense on SWI — SWI cannot reliably distinguish them without CT correlationTrauma (microhemorrhages); NF1 foci; metabolic iron; venous thrombosis; vascular malformations
Coronal T1/T2Are the hippocampi normal?Hippocampal volume/signal symmetry; mesial temporal sclerosis; pituitary height and signal; cavernous sinuses; optic nerves; temporal hornsNormal hippocampal asymmetry of up to ~10% volume difference is common — asymmetry alone is insufficient for MTS without signal changeEpilepsy workup; MTS; pituitary lesions; optic pathway glioma
MRA (TOF)Are the major vessels patent?Circle of Willis completeness; ICA, vertebral, basilar patency; arterial caliber; focal stenosis or occlusion; aneurysmTOF MRA overestimates stenosis at slow-flow segments — vessel dropout does not always mean occlusion. Confirm with CTA or DSA if clinically critical.Stroke; vasculopathy; moyamoya; arterial dissection
The Myelination Timetable — A Practical Cheat Sheet
Birth → 3 months: T1-bright in posterior limb internal capsule (PLIC), posterior corpus callosum, cerebellar WM. T2 still bright (unmyelinated) almost everywhere.
3–6 months: T1 myelination advances anteriorly (ALIC, genu CC). T2 begins to darken in PLIC and cerebellar WM.
6–12 months: T2 darkening spreads. Subcortical WM begins to myelinate T2 centripetally (central → peripheral).
12–18 months: Most central WM myelinated on T2. Subcortical U-fibers last.
18–24 months: Adult-like appearance. Residual T2 signal in peritrigonal WM (normal terminal zones — do not over-read as leukodystrophy).
Key rule: T1 leads T2 in the first year. If T1 looks myelinated but T2 does not, that is normal. If neither looks myelinated for age, suspect pathology.

Leukodystrophy Pattern Recognition

Leukodystrophies are genetically determined disorders of white matter — distinguished from acquired white matter disease by their symmetric, progressive, and anatomically patterned involvement. The single most useful first step is to identify the predominant WM pattern, which narrows the differential substantially before genetic testing is sent. The key distinction is between demyelination (myelin actively destroyed — typically progressive with MRI change) and hypomyelination (myelin never formed normally — T2-bright WM that does not progress on serial imaging).

Key Questions When Approaching a Leukodystrophy
Anterior or posterior predominance? (narrows differential immediately)
Are the subcortical U-fibers involved early, or spared? (U-fiber involvement early is unusual and specific)
Is there cavitation or cystic change within the WM? (VWM, Canavan, MLC, AGS)
Are the basal ganglia or thalami involved? (Alexander, Krabbe, Canavan, Wilson — not a leukodystrophy — AGS)
Is there corticospinal tract tracking? (X-ALD, Krabbe, MLD, LBSL)
Is calcification present? (AGS, Cockayne, TORCH — always get SWI and consider CT)
Is this demyelination (progressive, destructive) or hypomyelination (static, never myelinated)?
Does the WM signal change follow the normal myelination map, just delayed — or is it in the wrong place?

Table 2. Leukodystrophy Pattern Recognition

PATTERNCHARACTERISTICSTHINK ABOUT
Anterior-predominant WMFrontal lobes affected early and most severely; posterior WM relatively sparedAlexander disease (frontal + basal ganglia + brainstem); Canavan (diffuse but often frontal-heavy + subcortical U-fibers early); MLC
Posterior-predominant WMParieto-occipital WM affected early; frontal spared until lateX-ALD (parieto-occipital + splenium of CC + corticospinal tracts — classic pattern); MLD (posterior > anterior); Krabbe (posterior WM + corticospinal tracts)
Central/periventricular WM, U-fibers sparedDeep periventricular WM involved; subcortical U-fibers preserved until lateMLD; Krabbe; X-ALD; PMD (hypomyelination); most demyelinating leukodystrophies early
U-fibers involved earlySubcortical arcuate fibers affected early — unusual and narrows differential significantlyCanavan (NAA accumulation → subcortical swelling); Alexander; MLC; L-2-hydroxyglutaric aciduria
Cystic change / cavitationAreas of frank cavitation or cyst formation within abnormal WMVWM (vanishing white matter — CSF-like signal replacing WM); Canavan (swelling → cavitation late); MLC; Aicardi-Goutières (calcification + WM cysts)
Tigroid / leopard-skin patternPatchy perivascular WM sparing within diffuse signal abnormalityMLD (metachromatic leukodystrophy) — classic tigroid pattern on T2
Basal ganglia + WMBasal ganglia signal change accompanying white matter diseaseAlexander (basal ganglia T2 signal, caudate/putamen); Krabbe (thalami — T2 hypointense from storage); Canavan (globi pallidi involvement); Wilson (not leukodystrophy but mimics)
Corticospinal tract involvementSignal change tracking the pyramidal tracts from corona radiata through posterior limb IC, brainstemX-ALD; Krabbe; MLD; LBSL (brainstem and spinal cord)
Calcification + WM signalBasal ganglia/WM calcification on SWI or CT alongside white matter T2 changeAicardi-Goutières syndrome (AGS) — calcification + WM cysts + interferon signature; Cockayne; TORCH
Hypomyelination (not demyelination)WM never myelinates normally — thin myelin, not actively destroyed; T2 hyperintensity without progression or cavitationPMD (Pelizaeus-Merzbacher, PLP1); 18q deletion; hypomyelination with atrophy of basal ganglia and cerebellum (HABC); distinguish from delayed myelination by lack of improvement over time

Table 3. Disease-Specific Leukodystrophy MRI Signatures

DISORDERGENE / MECHANISMMRI SIGNATUREKEY DISTINGUISHING FEATURECLINICAL CLUERADIOPAEDIA
X-ALDABCD1 / VLCFA peroxisomal oxidation failureParieto-occipital WM + splenium CC; enhancement at leading edge (inflammatory front); corticospinal tract involvementEnhancement at active demyelinating front is hallmark — not seen in most other leukodystrophiesBoys; behavioral/school decline; adrenal insufficiency; NBS in KYCase →
KrabbeGALC / galactocerebrosidase deficiency; psychosine toxicityPosterior WM + corticospinal tracts; T2 hypointense thalami (storage); later cerebellar WM and atrophy; early: periventricular signalT2 hypointense thalami (dense galactosylceramide storage) — unusual finding in a leukodystrophyInfantile: rapid regression, irritability, peripheral neuropathy; NBS in KYCase →
AlexanderGFAP gain-of-function / astrocyte dysfunctionFrontal-predominant WM + caudate/putamen signal; periventricular garland enhancement; brainstem signal; megalencephalyFrontal predominance + basal ganglia signal + periventricular enhancement = nearly pathognomonicMegalencephaly; infantile-onset seizures; later bulbar dysfunctionCase →
CanavanASPA / NAA accumulation → subcortical edemaDiffuse WM + early subcortical U-fiber involvement; globi pallidi T2 signal; restricted diffusion in acute phase; megalencephalyNAA peak on MRS (markedly elevated) — should prompt Canavan specificallyMegalencephaly; hypotonia; Ashkenazi Jewish background; MRS is keyCase →
VWMEIF2B1-5 / integrated stress response failure → vanishing oligodendrocytesDiffuse WM T2 hyperintensity progressing to CSF-like signal (WM literally disappears); relative cortical and U-fiber sparing earlyWM becomes isointense to CSF on all sequences — literally vanishing on serial imagingStress-triggered episodes of rapid neurological decline (fever, trauma); ovarian failure in females (ovarioleukodystrophy)Case →
AGSTREX1, RNASEH2A-C, SAMHD1, ADAR, IFIH1 / innate immune dysregulation; type-I interferon signatureBilateral basal ganglia calcification (SWI/CT); WM T2 signal + cystic change; cerebral atrophy; sometimes brainstem signalCalcification + WM cysts + CSF interferon-alpha elevation + lymphocytosis = AGS until proven otherwiseCongenital or early infantile onset; mimics TORCH; chilblains; interferon signature on gene expression; no infectious cause foundCase →
Terminal Zones — Do Not Over-Read
Peritrigonal (posterior periventricular) T2 hyperintensity is a normal finding in children up to approximately 6–8 years of age.
These represent late-myelinating regions (terminal zones) — they are NOT leukodystrophy.
They are symmetric, do not have corresponding T1 or DWI signal change, do not progress, and do not involve U-fibers.
If uncertain: serial MRI. Terminal zones shrink over time. Leukodystrophy does not.

Malformations of Cortical Development (MCD)

MCDs arise from disruption of the three sequential stages of cortical formation: proliferation (germinal zone, weeks 7–16), migration (radial/tangential, weeks 12–24), and organization/postmigration (synaptogenesis, weeks 24–postnatal). The earlier the disruption, the more severe and widespread the malformation. This mechanistic framework is worth knowing because it maps onto the imaging phenotype: migration defects produce heterotopia and lissencephaly; organization defects produce PMG and FCD.

Technical Requirements for MCD Evaluation
Standard clinical MRI protocols frequently miss subtle MCDs. If MCD is suspected clinically (focal epilepsy, focal deficit, DD of uncertain etiology), request an epilepsy protocol MRI.
Epilepsy protocol essentials: 3T magnet; 1 mm isotropic T1 (MPRAGE/SPGR) for surface rendering and volumetrics; thin-slice coronal T2 and FLAIR perpendicular to hippocampal axis; thin axial T2 through cortex.
FCD type I is MRI-negative or near-invisible on standard sequences in a significant proportion of cases.
Surface rendering (3D reconstruction) dramatically improves detection of subtle PMG, FCD, and focal pachygyria.

Table 4. Malformations of Cortical Development — Imaging and Clinical Features

MALFORMATIONMECHANISM (STAGE)MRI FINDINGSKEY FEATURES / PITFALLSRADIOPAEDIA
Lissencephaly / pachygyriaNeuronal migration failure (weeks 12–16)Smooth brain surface; thickened cortex (10–20 mm, normal ~4 mm); 4-layer cortex on high-resolution T1; hourglass or figure-8 brain on axialGraded 1–6 (Dobyns scale). LIS1: posterior>anterior gradient. DCX: anterior>posterior. Anterior-only pachygyria may be subtle — check cortical thickness on thin-slice coronal T1.Case →
Band heterotopiaPartial neuronal migration arrest (DCX, LIS1)Smooth band of gray matter between ventricle and cortex, separated by thin WM layers; overlying cortex often normal or mildly simplifiedCan be very subtle on standard sequences — thin-slice coronal T1 is essential. Females with DCX mutations. Associated with epilepsy even when subtle.Case →
Periventricular nodular heterotopia (PNH)Failure of neuronal departure from germinal matrix (FLNA, ARFGEF2)Nodules of gray matter signal lining the lateral ventricle walls; isointense to cortex on ALL sequences (key — they follow gray matter, not WM, on every sequence)Must be isointense to gray matter on T1, T2, and FLAIR. Tubers (TSC) are NOT isointense to cortex — they are hyperintense on FLAIR. FLNA: X-linked, females; periventricular + cardiovascular anomalies.Case →
Polymicrogyria (PMG)Post-migrational cortical organization failure (many causes: TORCH, perfusion, PIK3R2, GPR56)Irregular cortical surface with too many too-small gyri; cortex appears thick on low-resolution but thin on high-resolution; T2 may show cortical signal irregularity; often perisylvianPMG mimics pachygyria on low-resolution MRI — thin-slice T1 resolves the irregular surface. Bilateral perisylvian PMG: pseudobulbar palsy + epilepsy. Unilateral: focal epilepsy, hemiplegia.Case →
Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD)Focal failure of cortical organization/maturation (MTOR pathway most common; FCD IIb = balloon cells)FCD I: subtle cortical thickening, blurring of gray-white junction — easy to miss. FCD II: transmantle sign (T2/FLAIR hyperintensity extending from cortex to ventricle); cortical thickening + blurred GWJ. FCD IIb: balloon cells; strongly epileptogenic.FCD I is frequently MRI-negative or near-invisible on standard sequences — if clinical suspicion, request epilepsy protocol (thin 1mm isotropic MPRAGE, surface rendering). The transmantle sign in FCD IIb is highly specific but not always present.Case →
SchizencephalyCleft lined by dysplastic cortex (gray matter–lined cleft traversing hemisphere)Open-lip: CSF-filled cleft communicating with ventricle, wide lips. Closed-lip: lips apposed, dimple in ventricular wall — easy to miss. Gray matter lines the cleft (distinguish from porencephaly which has WM walls).Closed-lip schizencephaly is a common miss — look for focal dimpling of the lateral ventricle and trace the cortex carefully. Bilateral: severe DD; unilateral: focal epilepsy, hemiplegia.Case →

Normal Variants vs. Pathology — High-Yield Distinctions

Table 5. Normal Variants vs. Pathological Mimics

ENTITYNORMAL VARIANT / BENIGNPATHOLOGICAL MIMICHOW TO DISTINGUISHRADIOPAEDIA
Mega cisterna magna vs. Dandy-Walker spectrumMega cisterna magna: enlarged posterior fossa CSF space, normal vermis and 4th ventricle, normal tentorium. Incidental, no intervention.Dandy-Walker malformation: vermian hypoplasia/rotation, enlarged 4th ventricle communicating with posterior fossa cyst, elevated tentorium. Associated with hydrocephalus and NDI.Key question: Is the vermis normal and fully formed? Is the 4th ventricle enlarged and communicating with the cyst? Elevated torcula (torcular-lambdoid inversion) = DWM. Blake's pouch cyst: enlarged retrocerebellar space + normal vermis + may have hydrocephalus (4th ventricle outlet obstruction).Case →
Prominent perivascular spaces vs. lacunar infarctsPVS: CSF-signal spaces following perforating vessels; isointense to CSF on ALL sequences including FLAIR (suppressed); no surrounding T2/FLAIR signal; common in basal ganglia, centrum semiovale, midbrain.Lacunar infarct: T2/FLAIR hyperintense rim; may show restricted diffusion acutely; associated WM disease; not perfectly CSF-signal on all sequences.FLAIR is the key sequence: PVS suppress completely; lacunes show a hyperintense rim. Giant PVS in the midbrain/basal ganglia can be dramatic in appearance but are still benign if they follow CSF signal on all sequences.Case →
Delayed myelination vs. leukodystrophyDelayed myelination: WM appears immature for age (T2-bright, T1-dark), but pattern and distribution are normal — just younger-appearing. No other signal abnormality. Follows normal myelination sequence (posterior→anterior, central→peripheral).Leukodystrophy: signal abnormality that does not follow normal myelination progression; may be patchy, cystic, involve U-fibers early, show atrophy, or fail to improve on serial imaging.Serial MRI is definitive: delayed myelination improves and normalizes. Leukodystrophy plateaus or progresses. Pattern matters: normal myelination is always central-to-peripheral and posterior-to-anterior — an anterior-only or U-fiber signal change is never normal.Case →
Benign external hydrocephalus of infancy (BEH)BEH: enlarged subarachnoid spaces (frontal > parietal), mildly enlarged ventricles, macrocephaly tracking above curve — but crossing percentiles slowly. Bridging cortical veins visible. Family history of macrocephaly common. Resolves by age 2–3.Subdural hygromas/hematomas (NAT, meningitis), communicating hydrocephalus from prior hemorrhage/infection, storage disorders (Canavan, MPS) causing true megalencephaly.Bridging veins should cross the enlarged subarachnoid space freely in BEH — if they appear stretched or there is blood products on SWI, consider NAT. Head circumference trajectory matters: BEH grows fast but decelerates; hydrocephalus continues to accelerate. MRI: cortex is normal in BEH.Case →
Cavum septum pellucidum / cavum vergaeCSP: fluid-filled space between the leaves of the septum pellucidum, between the frontal horns. Present in nearly all premature infants, closes postnatally. Cavum vergae: posterior extension of CSP. Both are normal variants when found incidentally.Rare: large CSP can cause obstructive hydrocephalus (colloid cyst-like obstruction — very rare). Septum pellucidum absence: look for lobar HPE or septo-optic dysplasia (absent SP + small optic nerves + cortical malformation).Isolated CSP/CV: reassure. Absent septum pellucidum is not the same as cavum SP — absent SP requires evaluation for HPE and SOD. Check optic nerve size and hypothalamic morphology if SP is absent.Case →
Tonsillar ectopia: Chiari I vs. incidentalIncidental tonsillar ectopia: tonsils 3–5 mm below foramen magnum in an asymptomatic child, no crowding of the foramen magnum, no syrinx, no CSF flow abnormality.Chiari I malformation: tonsils ≥5–6 mm below FM with crowding, distorted CSF flow at craniocervical junction, syrinx (present in ~30–70%), symptoms (headache worse with Valsalva, myelopathy, cranial nerve symptoms).The number (5 mm) is a guide, not a threshold — symptoms + syrinx + CSF flow abnormality matter more than mm. A syrinx requires neurosurgical referral regardless of tonsillar position. Phase-contrast CSF flow MRI can demonstrate obstruction. Asymptomatic tonsils at 5–6 mm with no syrinx: neurosurgery opinion reasonable but surgery not always needed.Case →
Arachnoid cystMost arachnoid cysts are incidental — CSF-signal on all sequences, no surrounding signal change, thin walls, no enhancement, no restricted diffusion.Epidermoid cyst: restricts diffusion (high DWI, low ADC) — critical distinction. Subdural hygroma: crescent-shaped, follows head trauma. Tumors with cystic components: enhancement, nodule, surrounding edema.DWI is the key sequence to distinguish arachnoid cyst (no restriction) from epidermoid (restriction). Most arachnoid cysts do not require treatment — management decisions hinge on symptoms and mass effect, not size alone. Middle fossa is the most common location.Case →
Pineal region cystSimple pineal cyst: round, CSF-signal, ≤10–12 mm, no solid nodule, no hydrocephalus — overwhelmingly benign. Extremely common (up to 40% of MRIs in adolescents).Pineal region tumor: pineocytoma, pineoblastoma, germ cell tumor (GCT). Features: solid or heterogeneous components, enhancement, >15 mm, obstructive hydrocephalus, Parinaud syndrome.If cyst is simple, <15 mm, no enhancement, no hydrocephalus, and no Parinaud — reassure. Parinaud syndrome (upgaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus, light-near dissociation) = evaluate for tumor urgently. AFP and β-hCG in serum and CSF for suspected GCT.Case →
Asymmetric hippocampiMinor hippocampal asymmetry (up to ~10% volume difference, no signal change) is common and does not require further workup in a non-epilepsy context.Mesial temporal sclerosis (MTS): unilateral hippocampal volume loss + T2/FLAIR hyperintensity + internal architecture loss (loss of striation on coronal T2). Associated with temporal lobe epilepsy.Signal change on T2/FLAIR is required for MTS — volume asymmetry alone is insufficient. Always use thin-slice coronal T2 and FLAIR perpendicular to the hippocampal long axis. Dual pathology (MTS + cortical dysplasia) occurs and may require careful review of both temporal lobes and the ipsilateral cortex.Case →
Practical Rules for Variants
Tonsillar position alone does not determine clinical significance — always note whether a syrinx is present and whether symptoms are consistent with Chiari.
Benign external hydrocephalus: the key safety question is whether bridging veins appear stretched or there are blood products — if either, consider non-accidental trauma.
Pineal cysts ≥15 mm, heterogeneous, or enhancing require neurosurgical evaluation regardless of symptoms.

Neonatal Brain Injury Patterns

Neonatal brain injury patterns reflect the interaction between the type and duration of the insult and the regional metabolic vulnerability at the gestational age of injury. Term and preterm brains are injured differently — because their most metabolically active and most vulnerable regions differ. Understanding this mapping between insult type, gestational age, and imaging pattern is the conceptual foundation for interpreting neonatal MRI.

MRI Timing in HIE — A Critical Practical Point
Optimal MRI timing for term HIE is 3–5 days of life: DWI restriction peaks and is most conspicuous at this window.
Early MRI (<24–48h) frequently UNDERESTIMATES injury — DWI restriction evolves over the first 24–72 hours, and a near-normal early MRI does not exclude significant injury.
By 7–10 days, DWI restriction pseudonormalizes (ADC rebounds) and injury may paradoxically appear less severe on DWI — T1 and T2 sequences become more informative at this stage.
Cooling (hypothermia therapy) delays the evolution of DWI changes slightly — MRI in cooled infants should be performed after rewarming when feasible.
MRI at term-equivalent age (TEA, ~36–40 weeks PMA) is the standard for preterm infants — performed before NICU discharge to characterize WM injury, IVH sequelae, and myelination.

Table 6. Neonatal Brain Injury Patterns

PATTERNPOPULATION / MECHANISMMRI FINDINGSPROGNOSTIC IMPLICATIONRADIOPAEDIA
Term HIE — Watershed (partial-prolonged injury)Term infant; partial prolonged hypoxia-ischemia (e.g., cord compression, placental insufficiency); watershed zones most vulnerableDWI restriction in parasagittal cortex and subcortical WM at watershed zones (anterior-middle and middle-posterior cerebral artery borders); relative sparing of deep gray and posterior fossaVariable outcome — depends on extent of watershed injury. Relatively preserved thalami and BG associated with better motor and cognitive prognosis than central pattern.Case →
Term HIE — Central/Profound (acute-profound injury)Term infant; sudden profound hypoxia-ischemia (e.g., acute cord prolapse, uterine rupture, profound bradycardia); deep gray structures most metabolically active and most vulnerableDWI restriction in bilateral thalami, lentiform nuclei, perirolandic cortex, hippocampi, dorsal brainstem; relative initial sparing of watershed zonesWorse prognosis than watershed pattern. Bilateral thalamic involvement + perirolandic injury associated with severe motor impairment (spastic quadriplegia, dyskinesia) and cognitive disability. Dorsal brainstem involvement predicts bulbar dysfunction.Case →
Preterm HIE — Periventricular leukomalacia (PVL)Premature infant (<32 weeks); periventricular WM most vulnerable (watershed zone in preterm); oligodendrocyte precursor vulnerabilityAcute: periventricular T1 hyperintensity (coagulative necrosis) ± echogenicity on US. Chronic: periventricular T2 hyperintensity, WM volume loss, irregular lateral ventricle margins (colpocephaly), thin posterior CC, delayed myelinationSeverity scales with extent of WM injury. Posterior periventricular WM loss → visual impairment (optic radiations). Diffuse WM injury → cognitive and motor impairment. Periventricular WM volume is a strong predictor of neurodevelopmental outcome.Case →
Germinal matrix hemorrhage / IVH (GMH-IVH)Premature infant; fragile germinal matrix vasculature; fluctuating CBF; coagulopathyGrade I: subependymal hemorrhage only. Grade II: IVH without ventricular distension. Grade III: IVH + ventricular distension. Grade IV (periventricular hemorrhagic infarction, PVHI): parenchymal involvement — venous infarction, NOT extension of IVH per se.Grade I–II: generally favorable. Grade III: hydrocephalus risk; variable NDI. PVHI (Grade IV): significant risk of hemiplegia and cognitive impairment ipsilateral to parenchymal injury. Post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus requires monitoring and may need intervention.Case →
